item.description:
A Chu space (Barr 1979, developed by Pratt 1999) over a value set LL is a triple (A,r,X)(A, r, X) where AA is a set of states (or objects), XX is a set of attributes (or observations), and r:A×X→Lr: A \times X \to L is an evaluation function.

Pratt (1999) proved that a formal context (G,M,I)(G, M, I) is a Chu space over {0,1}\{0, 1\}, with GG as states, MM as attributes, and II as evaluation. Many-valued formal contexts are Chu spaces over the value set LL.

The contrapositive of an implication p→qp o q is ¬q→¬p eg q o eg p. In classical logic, these are equivalent: proving p→qp o q is the same as proving ¬q→¬p eg q o eg p.

On the four-valued bilattice, the simple contrapositive fails: ∼(a⊙b)≠∼b⇒∼a\mathord{\sim}(a \odot b) eq \mathord{\sim} b \Rightarrow \mathord{\sim} a in general. Negation does not commute with the residuated operations.

The Nelson implication repairs this by incorporating both the direct and contrapositive simultaneously: x⇒Ny=(x⊃y)∧(∼y⊃∼x)x \Rightarrow_N y = (x \supset y) \wedge (\mathord{\sim} y \supset \mathord{\sim} x). The Nelson identity holds on the bilattice even though the simple contrapositive does not. Negation-aware iterated implication is sound; naive contrapositive is not.

A logic is decidable if there exists an algorithm that determines, for any formula in the logic, whether the formula is valid — and the algorithm terminates in finite time on every input.

Decidability means the logic can be mechanized. A decidable logic admits a decision procedure: a program that takes a formula and returns yes or no, always halting.

Classical propositional logic is decidable (truth tables). Classical first-order logic is not (Church-Turing). Modal logic S5 is decidable. The Lambek calculus is decidable (Pentus 1993).

A formal context (Ganter & Wille 1999) is a triple (G,M,I)(G, M, I) where GG is a set of objects, MM is a set of attributes, and I⊆G×MI \subseteq G \times M (or I:G×M→LI: G \times M \to L for a many-valued context) is an incidence relation.

I(g,m)I(g, m) states that object gg has attribute mm. In the binary case, this is yes/no. In the many-valued case, I(g,m)I(g, m) takes a value from a structured set LL.

The derivation operators on a formal context define a Galois connection between subsets of objects and subsets of attributes. The fixed points of this connection — pairs (A,B)(A, B) with A′=BA' = B and B′=AB' = A — are the formal concepts. They form a concept lattice.

A Heyting algebra is a bounded distributive lattice (H,∧,∨,0,1)(H, \wedge, \vee, 0, 1) with an additional operation — the Heyting implication → o — satisfying:

c≤(a→b)if and only ifc∧a≤bc \leq (a o b) \quad ext{if and only if}\quad c \wedge a \leq b

Implication is right adjoint to meet. This residuation law makes a Heyting algebra an integral commutative residuated lattice where the product is meet.

Heyting algebras are the algebraic semantics of intuitionistic logic, where excluded middle (a∨¬a=1a \vee eg a = 1) does not hold in general. A Boolean algebra is a Heyting algebra where every element has a complement (¬¬a=a eg eg a = a). The open sets of a topological space form a complete Heyting algebra.

Kripke semantics (Kripke 1963) interprets modal logic over relational structures called Kripke frames.

A Kripke frame is a pair (W,R)(W, R) where WW is a set of worlds and R⊆W×WR \subseteq W \times W is an accessibility relation. A Kripke model adds a valuation VV assigning truth values to propositions at each world.

The modal operators are defined by the accessibility relation:

  • □p\Box p holds at world ww iff pp holds at every world RR-accessible from ww.
  • ◊p\Diamond p holds at ww iff pp holds at some RR-accessible world.

Different conditions on RR yield different modal logics: reflexive gives T, reflexive and transitive gives S4, equivalence relation gives S5.

The Lambek calculus (Lambek 1958) formalizes how a functor — a predicate, a verb, any expression that takes arguments — combines with its arguments to produce a complete expression.

A product operation ⋅\cdot combines two expressions. The calculus introduces two residuals:

a⋅b≤ciffb≤a\ciffa≤c/ba \cdot b \leq c \quad\text{iff}\quad b \leq a \backslash c \quad\text{iff}\quad a \leq c / b

The left residual a\ca \backslash c asks: what must combine with aa on the right to yield something below cc? The right residual c/bc / b asks: what must combine with bb on the left? These two residuals exhaust the modes of functor-argument interaction. The product has two argument positions, so there are exactly two residuals.

Linear logic (Girard 1987) is a logic where premises are resources: using a premise consumes it. In classical logic, a hypothesis can be used zero, one, or many times. In linear logic, each hypothesis is used exactly once unless explicitly marked as reusable.

The fundamental connectives are:

  • Tensor (⊗\otimes): multiplicative conjunction. A⊗BA \otimes B means “I have both AA and BB as resources.” Producing A⊗BA \otimes B requires producing both.
  • Linear implication (⊸\multimap): A⊸BA \multimap B means “consuming one AA produces one BB.”
  • Par (\parr): multiplicative disjunction. Dual to tensor.
  • Bang (!A!A): “as many copies of AA as needed.” Recovers classical logic’s unlimited use.

Tensor and linear implication form an adjunction: ⊗⊣⊸\otimes \dashv \multimap. This is the fundamental adjunction of linear logic, analogous to the residuation a⋅b≤ca \cdot b \leq c iff b≤a→cb \leq a \to c in a residuated lattice.

Modal logic extends classical logic with two operators: necessity □\Box (“it must be the case that”) and possibility ◊\Diamond (“it could be the case that”). These are dual: □p=¬◊¬p\Box p = \neg \Diamond \neg p.

The standard semantics is Kripke semantics (Kripke 1963): a model consists of a set of worlds WW, an accessibility relation RR on WW, and a valuation assigning truth values to propositions at each world. □p\Box p holds at world ww iff pp holds at every world accessible from ww. ◊p\Diamond p holds at ww iff pp holds at some accessible world.

The Nelson implication is an implication operation on bilattices and twist products that incorporates both direct and contrapositive reasoning simultaneously:

x⇒Ny=(x⊃y)∧(∼y⊃∼x)x \Rightarrow_N y = (x \supset y) \wedge (\mathord{\sim} y \supset \mathord{\sim} x)

where ⊃\supset is the weak (Heyting) implication and ∼\sim is negation.

Chajda & Langer (2021) established the Nelson implication as a second adjoint pair on twist products, alongside the Busaniche-Cignoli residuation. It satisfies the Nelson identity:

(x⇒(x⇒y))∧k(∼y⇒(∼y⇒∼x))=x⇒y(x \Rightarrow (x \Rightarrow y)) \wedge_k (\mathord{\sim} y \Rightarrow (\mathord{\sim} y \Rightarrow \mathord{\sim} x)) = x \Rightarrow y

On the four-valued bilattice 2⊗2\mathbf{2} \otimes \mathbf{2}, the simple contrapositive fails: ∼(a⊙b)≠∼b⇒∼a\mathord{\sim}(a \odot b) \neq \mathord{\sim} b \Rightarrow \mathord{\sim} a in general. The Nelson form — which tests both the direct implication and the contrapositive at once — is sound. Any modal calculus on bilattice-valued fibers must use the Nelson implication for negation-aware reasoning, not the naive contrapositive.

Priestley duality (Priestley 1970) is a dual equivalence between the category of bounded distributive lattices and the category of Priestley spaces — compact topological spaces equipped with a compatible partial order.

Every bounded distributive lattice LL has a Priestley dual: the space of prime filters of LL, topologized and ordered. Every Priestley space determines a lattice (its clopen upsets). These constructions are inverse to each other up to isomorphism.

The duality gives every lattice a geometric dual. Algebraic properties of the lattice translate to topological properties of the space and vice versa.

A substructural logic is a logic that restricts one or more of the structural rules assumed in classical logic:

  • Contraction (A,A⊢BA, A \vdash B implies A⊢BA \vdash B): a hypothesis can be used more than once. Dropping this gives resource sensitivity.
  • Weakening (A⊢BA \vdash B implies A,C⊢BA, C \vdash B): unused hypotheses are harmless. Dropping this gives relevance.
  • Exchange (A,B⊢CA, B \vdash C implies B,A⊢CB, A \vdash C): order doesn’t matter. Dropping this gives non-commutativity.

Linear logic drops contraction and weakening: each hypothesis is used exactly once. The Lambek calculus drops all three: hypotheses are ordered, used exactly once, and cannot be reordered.

item.title: Heyting Implication
item.description:
Let HH be a Heyting algebra.

Definition. The Heyting implication (or relative pseudocomplement) is the binary operation →\to on HH defined by

a→b  =  max⁡{c∈H:a∧c≤b}.a \to b \;=\; \max\{c \in H : a \wedge c \leq b\}.

Equivalently, →\to is characterized by the residuation law: for all a,b,c∈Ha, b, c \in H,

c≤(a→b)⟺a∧c≤b.c \leq (a \to b) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad a \wedge c \leq b.

Proposition. For each a∈Ha \in H, the functor (a∧−)(a \wedge -) is left adjoint to (a→−)(a \to -). That is, ∧\wedge and →\to form an adjunction in the poset category HH.

item.title: Accessibility Relation
item.description:
Let WW be a set (of possible worlds).

Definition. An accessibility relation on WW is a binary relation R⊆W×WR \subseteq W \times W. We write wRvwRv to mean (w,v)∈R(w, v) \in R, read as “vv is accessible from ww.”

The accessibility relation determines the semantics of the modal operators. In a Kripke model (W,R,V)(W, R, V):

  • Necessity: □φ\Box\varphi holds at ww iff φ\varphi holds at every vv with wRvwRv.
  • Possibility: ◊φ\Diamond\varphi holds at ww iff φ\varphi holds at some vv with wRvwRv.

Proposition (Frame correspondence). The properties of RR determine which modal system the Kripke frame (W,R)(W, R) validates:

item.title: Connective
item.description:
A connective is a logical operation that builds compound propositions from simpler ones. “It is raining and it is cold” combines two propositions using the connective and. “If it rains, then the ground is wet” uses the connective if-then. “It is not raining” uses negation. Connectives are the glue of logical reasoning — they determine how the truth of a compound statement depends on the truth of its parts.

The standard connectives are five:

item.title: Formula
item.description:
Let L\mathcal{L} be a formal language with a signature Σ\Sigma consisting of constant symbols, function symbols (each with a specified arity), relation symbols (each with a specified arity), and a countable set of variables.

Definition. The set Form(L)\mathrm{Form}(\mathcal{L}) of well-formed formulas (or simply formulas) of L\mathcal{L} is the smallest set satisfying:

  1. Atomic formulas. If RR is an nn-ary relation symbol and t1,…,tnt_1, \ldots, t_n are terms of L\mathcal{L}, then R(t1,…,tn)∈Form(L)R(t_1, \ldots, t_n) \in \mathrm{Form}(\mathcal{L}). In particular, ⊤,⊥∈Form(L)\top, \bot \in \mathrm{Form}(\mathcal{L}).
  2. Connectives. If φ,ψ∈Form(L)\varphi, \psi \in \mathrm{Form}(\mathcal{L}), then (φ∧ψ)(\varphi \wedge \psi), (φ∨ψ)(\varphi \vee \psi), (φ→ψ)(\varphi \to \psi), and (¬φ)(\neg\varphi) are in Form(L)\mathrm{Form}(\mathcal{L}).
  3. Quantifiers. If φ∈Form(L)\varphi \in \mathrm{Form}(\mathcal{L}) and xx is a variable, then (∀x. φ)(\forall x.\, \varphi) and (∃x. φ)(\exists x.\, \varphi) are in Form(L)\mathrm{Form}(\mathcal{L}).

A term of L\mathcal{L} is built from variables and constant symbols by iterated application of function symbols.

item.title: Kripke Frame
item.description:
Let WW be a nonempty set.

Definition. A Kripke frame is a pair F=(W,R)\mathcal{F} = (W, R), where WW is a set of possible worlds and R⊆W×WR \subseteq W \times W is an accessibility relation on WW.

A frame carries no information about which propositions hold at which worlds. Adding a valuation V ⁣:Prop→P(W)V \colon \mathrm{Prop} \to \mathcal{P}(W) yields a Kripke model (W,R,V)(W, R, V).

Definition. A modal formula φ\varphi is valid on the frame F\mathcal{F}, written F⊨φ\mathcal{F} \models \varphi, if for every valuation VV and every w∈Ww \in W, (W,R,V),w⊨φ(W, R, V), w \models \varphi.

item.title: Kripke Model
item.description:
Let F=(W,R)\mathcal{F} = (W, R) be a Kripke frame.

Definition. A Kripke model on F\mathcal{F} is a triple M=(W,R,V)M = (W, R, V), where V ⁣:Prop→P(W)V \colon \mathrm{Prop} \to \mathcal{P}(W) is a valuation assigning to each atomic proposition pp the set of worlds at which pp is true.

Definition. The satisfaction relation M,w⊨φM, w \models \varphi (read “φ\varphi is true at ww in MM”) is defined recursively on modal formulas:

  • M,w⊨pM, w \models p iff w∈V(p)w \in V(p).
  • M,w⊨φ∧ψM, w \models \varphi \wedge \psi iff M,w⊨φM, w \models \varphi and M,w⊨ψM, w \models \psi.
  • M,w⊨φ∨ψM, w \models \varphi \vee \psi iff M,w⊨φM, w \models \varphi or M,w⊨ψM, w \models \psi.
  • M,w⊨¬φM, w \models \neg\varphi iff M,w⊭φM, w \not\models \varphi.
  • M,w⊨φ→ψM, w \models \varphi \to \psi iff M,w⊭φM, w \not\models \varphi or M,w⊨ψM, w \models \psi.
  • M,w⊨□φM, w \models \Box\varphi iff for all vv with wRvwRv, M,v⊨φM, v \models \varphi. (Necessity requires truth at all accessible worlds.)
  • M,w⊨◊φM, w \models \Diamond\varphi iff there exists vv with wRvwRv such that M,v⊨φM, v \models \varphi. (Possibility requires truth at some accessible world.)

A formula φ\varphi is satisfiable if M,w⊨φM, w \models \varphi for some MM and some ww. It is valid if M,w⊨φM, w \models \varphi for every MM and every ww.

item.title: Modal Operator
item.description:
Let HH be a Heyting algebra.

Definition. A modal operator on HH is a monotone map □ ⁣:H→H\square \colon H \to H satisfying additional axioms that depend on the intended modality. The two fundamental modal operators are:

  1. A necessity operator (□\Box): a closure operator on HH, i.e., a map □ ⁣:H→H\Box \colon H \to H satisfying

    • a≤□aa \leq \Box a (extensive),
    • a≤b⇒□a≤□ba \leq b \Rightarrow \Box a \leq \Box b (monotone),
    • □□a=□a\Box\Box a = \Box a (idempotent).

  2. A possibility operator (◊\Diamond): an interior operator on HH, i.e., the order-dual of a closure operator, satisfying ◊a≤a\Diamond a \leq a, monotonicity, and ◊◊a=◊a\Diamond\Diamond a = \Diamond a.

item.title: Modal System
item.description:
Let (A,□)(A, \Box) be a modal algebra, i.e., a Boolean algebra AA equipped with a unary operator □ ⁣:A→A\Box \colon A \to A satisfying □⊤=⊤\Box\top = \top and □(a∧b)=□a∧□b\Box(a \wedge b) = \Box a \wedge \Box b (normality).

Definition. A modal system (or normal modal logic) Λ\Lambda is a set of modal formulas containing all propositional tautologies and the axiom

K ⁣:  □(φ→ψ)→(□φ→□ψ),\mathbf{K}\colon\; \Box(\varphi \to \psi) \to (\Box\varphi \to \Box\psi),

closed under modus ponens, uniform substitution, and the necessity rule (if ⊢φ\vdash \varphi then ⊢□φ\vdash \Box\varphi). A modal system is identified with the class of modal algebras validating its theorems.

item.title: Model
item.description:
A model is a mathematical structure that makes a set of logical sentences true. It is the bridge between syntax (the formal sentences of a theory) and semantics (what those sentences are about). The axioms of group theory are sentences in a formal language. The integers under addition are a model of those axioms — a concrete mathematical object in which every axiom holds true.

The concept solves a fundamental problem: formal logic manipulates symbols according to rules, but symbols by themselves do not refer to anything. A model gives them reference. The symbol “0” becomes the number zero. The symbol “+” becomes the addition operation. The sentence “∀x (x+0=x)\forall x\, (x + 0 = x)” becomes a claim about actual numbers — and in the model of integers under addition, that claim is true.

item.title: Necessity
item.description: Necessity is the modality of what must be the case — what holds not just as things happen to be, but in every relevant way things could be. “2 + 2 = 4” is necessarily true: there is no possible world in which it fails. “It is raining in London” is contingently true (or false): it depends on circumstances that could have been otherwise. The concept of necessity, and its dual possibility, are the central subjects of modal logic.

item.title: Possibility
item.description:
Let HH be a Heyting algebra equipped with a closure operator j ⁣:H→Hj \colon H \to H.

Definition. The possibility operator (or interior operator) dual to jj is the map ◊ ⁣:H→H\Diamond \colon H \to H defined by

◊a  =  ⋁{c∈Hj:c≤a},\Diamond a \;=\; \bigvee \{c \in H^j : c \leq a\},

where Hj={c∈H:j(c)=c}H^j = \{c \in H : j(c) = c\} is the set of jj-closed elements. Equivalently, ◊a\Diamond a is the largest jj-closed element below aa.

In a Kripke model (W,R,V)(W, R, V), the semantic clause is:

item.title: Possible World
item.description: A possible world is a complete way things could be — a total state of affairs, specifying the truth value of every relevant proposition. The actual world is one possible world. A world where it rained yesterday in London but not in Paris is another. A world where the laws of physics are the same but Napoleon won at Waterloo is yet another. Possible worlds are the semantic foundation of modal logic: they give precise meaning to words like “necessarily,” “possibly,” “must,” and “might.”

item.title: Proposition
item.description: A proposition is a statement that is capable of being true or false. “It is raining” is a proposition. “2 + 2 = 4” is a proposition. “Close the door” is not — it is a command, not something that has a truth value. The concept is one of the oldest in logic: Aristotle defined it (as apophansis) in De Interpretatione as a sentence that can be affirmed or denied.

item.title: Quantifier
item.description:
Let C\mathcal{C} be a category with finite limits, and let π ⁣:X×D→X\pi \colon X \times D \to X be the projection morphism for objects X,D∈CX, D \in \mathcal{C}.

Definition. A quantifier along π\pi is a functor adjoint to the pullback (substitution) functor π∗ ⁣:Sub(X)→Sub(X×D)\pi^* \colon \mathrm{Sub}(X) \to \mathrm{Sub}(X \times D), where Sub(−)\mathrm{Sub}(-) denotes the poset of subobjects. Specifically:

  1. The existential quantifier ∃π ⁣:Sub(X×D)→Sub(X)\exists_\pi \colon \mathrm{Sub}(X \times D) \to \mathrm{Sub}(X) is the left adjoint of π∗\pi^*:

    ∃π(S)≤T⟺S≤π∗(T).\exists_\pi(S) \leq T \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad S \leq \pi^*(T).

  2. The universal quantifier ∀π ⁣:Sub(X×D)→Sub(X)\forall_\pi \colon \mathrm{Sub}(X \times D) \to \mathrm{Sub}(X) is the right adjoint of π∗\pi^*:
